name: Lanthanum
name_origin: From Greek 'lanthanein' meaning 'to lie hidden', as it was concealed in cerium ore.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Relatively abundant rare-earth element (~39 ppm in crust); found in monazite and bastnaesite ores together with other light lanthanides.

compounds.name: Lanthanum(III) oxide
compounds.summary: White solid used in optical glasses, ceramics, and catalysts; raises the refractive index of camera lenses.

compounds.name: Lanthanum(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Lewis acid catalyst used in organic synthesis and as a precursor for other lanthanum compounds.

compounds.name: Lanthanum(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Ionic conductor used in fluoride-selective ion electrodes and phosphors.

compounds.name: Lanthanum hexaboride
compounds.summary: High-performance thermionic emitter used in electron microscopes and electron-beam lithography cathodes.

uses: Carbon-arc lamp electrodes for studio lighting and projector lamps
High-refractive-index glass for camera and telescope lenses
Misch metal alloys for cigarette lighter flints
Nickel-metal hydride (NiMH) battery hydrogen storage alloys
Phosphate binder (lanthanum carbonate) for treating hyperphosphatemia in dialysis patients
reactivity_summary: Reactive silvery-white metal that tarnishes rapidly in moist air to form an oxide coating; reacts slowly with cold water and rapidly with hot water and acids to release hydrogen.
isotopes_summary: Lanthanum has two naturally occurring isotopes: stable La-139 (~99.91%) and primordial radioactive La-138 (half-life 1.05x10^11 years). Numerous synthetic isotopes are known, with La-137 (~6x10^4 years) the longest-lived synthetic.
